dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for a cardiac arrest at a residence in [CITY].
Hi, I'm Morgan, an EMT with the rescue squad. Can you tell me what happened here?
We found him not breathing and unresponsive in his bedroom. I called 911 immediately.
He doesn't respond to your voice, right? He's not opening his eyes at all.
Unresponsive to voice or touch. GCS appears to be 3.
Let's eliminate any potential musculoskeletal injury here before we move. Do you notice any other signs of awareness or movement?
No response at all. No movement or reaction to stimuli.
We need to assess his airway now. I'll position the head. Clear?
No visible chest movement. No audible breath sounds. Respirations are absent.
No spontaneous effort. No air movement observed. Resp 0 confirmed.
No carotid pulse detected at all by palpation.
Pulse is absent. Skin is warm, though, with no cyanosis.
SpO2 zero-no oxygen saturation detected.
Blood pressure is undetectable. No readings obtained.
Monitor is showing asystole. No cardiac activity present. EKG confirmed.
Quick blood glucose check. Unreadable-likely very low.
Pain not applicable. The patient shows no sign of discomfort.
Can you tell me when you last saw him? Was he awake?
Last saw him around 3 in the morning. He was breathing and sleeping.
Any history of seizures or other conditions? His friend may have more info.
He has a history of seizures, recently saw him in the ED at [CITY].
No trauma detected. No drug paraphernalia found in the room.
Let's clear the airway and maintain an open position. Apply a jaw thrust if needed.
Initiation of mechanical chest compressions. Let's start with the AutoPulse.
BVM with 100% oxygen delivered at high flow. Manual compressions ongoing.
RFD has already begun CPR with an AED. No shock advised.
45mm IO line placed in the right humeral head. Bone marrow aspirated. Good.
That's secure for drug pushes. Next step: Epinephrine.
Administering first dose of Epinephrine 1 mg via IO line.
Need to ensure airway patency. Time for oral intubation.
Using video laryngoscopy and bougie. Clear lumen. Attempting intubation now.
Visualize the vocal cords. Size 8.0 tube placed. Confirming placement.
Placement marker at level of the teeth. Bilateral breath sounds. Secure.
Second round of Epinephrine 1 mg IO on the clock at 09:02.
Second glucose reading now. Capture this before, it's going down.
Blood sugar drop-only 32. Trend is downwards.
Need to bring blood sugar up. A 100 mL D10 bolus infusing now.
Rhythm check done at 02 minutes. Asystole still shown.
Pulse is zero. Blood pressure unobtainable. Ongoing asystole. Resp remains 0.
Clearing secretions from the ET tube. Suction has been effective.
Third dose of Epinephrine administered at 09:07, 1mg.
Rechecking vital signs. No improvement observed.
Rhythm check again-still asystole.
Final dose of Epinephrine-1mg administered-now confirming.
Dr. Sauer on the line at [COMPANY_NAME]. Reports he's seen no vital signs over the last 20 minutes.
[COMPANY_NAME] approves discontinuation of resuscitation. Protocol exited per physician.
Scene secured for crime unit. No transport necessary-patient is deceased.
Time of death confirmed at 09:20. No cardiac rhythm for over 20 continuous minutes.
Scene will be handed off to officer. He's been technically dead for quite some time now.
Documentation concluded. Remain on scene for police brief.
Scene secure. No deviations in findings from narrative or ePCR.
He took Topamax for seizures. Seemed congested and reported chills recently.
Current meds include Topamax. No known drug allergies.
No signs of lividity or post-mortem changes. Confirmed dead per physician.
Jaw is loose. Confirmed per documentation for deceased.
No signs of drug paraphernalia beyond what's strictly documented.
Continuous assessments confirm no airway improvement following intubation.
Drugs administered per protocol. No evidence of response after 20 minutes.
Still asystole. No breathing. Protocol is consistent with arrest.
No oxygen saturation, pulse, or respiratory effort detected.
Based on the narrative, he had a seizure last week, postponed continued care.
ET tube secure; breath sounds checked and confirmed bilateral.
Confirming all time-stamped actions aligned with written ePCR documentation.
Final dose of Epinephrine on file at 09:16.
Scene has been tranquil since death declaration. Police will collect input and secure.
No evidence of illegal activity or unresolved concerns beyond this life event.
Transport to ER not needed. Case is closed for field responders.
No family members present. His friend provided narrative for completion.
Doctor's orders were followed. Full documentation finalized.
No impending removal of airway. Tube left in situ as part of field documentation.
Final SpO2 remains 0. Fully consistent with deceased status.
Police are now assuming control. Our job here is complete.
IO site remained dry, no swelling or complications noted during observation.
Scene clean and no hazards. No clear signs of death related to trauma or poison.
Vital signs reflect overall lack of life signs. Consistent with medical report.
No pharmacological response. No pulse. No breathing. No rhythm.
Time remains critical for timeliness in documentation.
No signs of tube occlusion post-intubation.
Apnea despite aggressive care. Code had been declared per [COMPANY_NAME].
Last dose of Epinephrine given just prior to physician review.
No ambiguity regarding lifelessness after 20 minutes of effort.
No financial burden for transport. Objectively unnecessary.
All four Epinephrine doses at 5-minute intervals fully documented.
No expected adverse effects following Epinephrine series.
Absolutely no movement or respiration in the last cycle.
D10 administered. No change in clinical status.
Patient remains in full arrest, no signs of spontaneous return.
Despite effective compression and ventilation, vital signs remain absent.
Suction maintained effectiveness prior to last drug push.
No pressure or odor evidence. No signs of GI bleed.
EMT and team have been thorough in execution and documentation.
All signs remain consistent at time of final determination.
No new findings added since last assessment.
Bystander accounts for 3-hour safety norm with baseline knowledge.
Overall environment stable, no hazards for responders.
No evidence connecting this to friend's initial meds or Topamax.
Scene remains fully secure. Release of duty to police.
Transport destination not required. All actions documented as per protocol.
